EdTech School Management Mobile-first

A school portal that
every stakeholder can use

MySkool Portal (MSP) is a mobile app that gives students and parents real-time access to the information that matters most — results, bills, attendance, and transcripts — without queuing at an admin office or waiting for a school newsletter. Designed for two distinct user types with genuinely different mental models, operating in the same institutional ecosystem.

Lead Product Designer
Students · Parents · School Admin
iOS · Android
4 months
Nigerian K–12 education sector
For Students
Results, bills, and transcripts. On a phone. In seconds.

View term results, check pending school fees, download transcripts as PDFs, track attendance, and access e-learning resources — without setting foot in an admin office. The dashboard surfaces the five things a student checks every term: score, standing, fees owed, last payment, and next action.

For Parents
All your wards across every school, one view.

Parents with children at multiple institutions see a unified dashboard. Switch between wards instantly, view each child's bills and results, make PTA levy payments, and receive school communications — without managing separate logins for each school.

For Institutions
Reduce admin load. Reduce missed payments.

When parents can see outstanding bills directly on their phones, payment rates improve. When students can check results themselves, admin queues shorten. MSP shifts the information burden from school staff to a self-service system — freeing admin to handle exceptions, not routine queries.

Security-first for shared-device contexts
The Problem

School information was stuck in the wrong places

For most Nigerian secondary schools, the flow of information between institution and family runs through a single bottleneck: the admin office. A parent who wants to know if school fees are overdue drives to school. A student who wants to see their term result waits for the principal to announce result-checking day. A parent of three children in three different schools keeps three different login portals — if the school even has one.

MSP was designed to eliminate these bottlenecks without requiring schools to overhaul their backend systems. The app is the interface — not the system of record. It surfaces what already exists in the school's database and makes it accessible to the people who need it most, when they need it.

The design challenge was not just information architecture. It was designing for a context where: users may have limited data access, devices are shared between family members, literacy levels vary, and trust in digital systems is still being established. Every screen had to earn its place.

Design constraints

What the design had to hold

  • 01
    Two user types, one codebase

    Students and parents share the same app but have fundamentally different dashboards, navigation structures, and data needs. The design had to feel purpose-built for each — not like a generic portal with a role toggle.

  • 02
    Multi-institution parent view

    A parent with children in different schools sees all their wards in a single dashboard. This required designing a switching model that felt native, not bolted on — and ensured billing and results data never crossed between institutions.

  • 03
    Security on shared devices

    In many households, one phone is used by multiple family members. The 6-digit PIN, biometric login, and 5-minute auto-lock exist because the people using this app are not always in private, on their own device, in a distraction-free environment.

  • 04
    Low-data resilience

    The app must be functional in conditions where a user has intermittent connectivity — either waiting for a page to load, or working from a previously cached state. Heavy imagery was avoided; data-dense screens favour text and lightweight charts over media.

  • 05
    Trust before data collection

    Nigerian users are appropriately sceptical of apps that ask for personal details before demonstrating value. The onboarding sequence leads with what the app does — results, bills, transcripts — before asking users to agree to terms or set a PIN.

Mobile App · Actual Product Screens

Six screens from the live MSP application

The screens below are the actual MySkool Portal product — not recreations. Every design decision reflected here — the blue primary (#3D52D5), teal attendance, violet ratings, the tab structure, the pill headers — comes directly from the shipped application.

Screen 01
Home Dashboard — layered information hierarchy

The welcome card establishes identity, the Male/Female tiles surface class composition, and the violet/teal rating and attendance cards use circular progress for immediate comprehension. The top-5 subjects bar chart closes the loop with subject-level performance detail.

Screen 02
Billings — RRR-based fee tracking

Each bill shows the RRR (Remita Retrieval Reference), amount, date, class badge, and pending status with a "Pay now" CTA. The Billing / Payment History tab split separates outstanding obligations from completed payments without separate navigation.

Screen 03
My Results — release-state driven list

Results are shown chronologically with a clear release status — "Not Released" in orange outline, "Released" in green. The three-tab control (All Students / Attendance / Results) keeps navigation within a unified Class management context.

Screen 04
Attendance — Present and Absent at maximum legibility

Large high-contrast tiles (blue for present, red for absent) communicate the ratio in under one second. The term dropdown allows historical comparison without cluttering the primary view.

Screen 05
My Subjects — 32 subjects, two categories

The teal subject count banner immediately tells students their load — 32 subjects split between 26 compulsory and 6 elective. Each row shows subject code and teacher name: the two pieces of information a student needs when matching a timetable or contacting staff.

Screen 06
More — consolidated secondary actions

The "More" tab consolidates Profile, Settings, Change Password, Get Transcript, E-Learning, FAQ's, Privacy Policy, and Log Out. Full-width white cards with leading icons and trailing chevrons — the pattern users understand as "tap to go deeper." Security-critical paths (Settings, Change Password) use the blue icon background as a visual signal.

Design Decisions

The thinking behind the choices

🔀

One app, two completely different dashboards

Students and parents share the same authentication flow but land on completely different home screens. The student dashboard centres on academic performance. The parent dashboard centres on wards and financial obligations. Attempting to merge them into a single "role-aware" screen would have produced a screen that felt appropriate to neither user type — so the architecture separates them entirely.

🎨

Colour as a grade signal, not decoration

Green (≥75%), amber (60–74%), red (below 60%) map directly to how Nigerian schools grade results — Distinction, Credit, Pass — giving students immediate grade comprehension without reading every percentage. The same system applies to bills: red for overdue, amber for due soon. Using colour semantically across both modules creates a product-wide visual language rather than scattered styling.

📊

CA and Exam score decomposition

Showing only the total result obscures where a student is losing marks. A student with 38/40 CA and 22/60 exam score has a fundamentally different study problem than one with 20/40 CA and 50/60. Decomposing the scores isn't more complex — it's more useful. This is data that exists in the school's system and students have always wanted to see; it was simply never surfaced.

🏫

Multi-institution wards without account sprawl

A parent with three children at two different schools would historically need two separate school portal logins, two fee payment workflows, and two result-checking processes. MSP's parent view unifies this at the app level — the parent has one account, sees all wards, and the data is partitioned by institution on the backend. The design ensures context never bleeds between institutions.

🔐

5-minute auto-lock as a design constraint, not just a feature

The auto-lock timeout wasn't just a security specification — it informed the entire information architecture. Screens are designed to be scannable in under 30 seconds because the auto-lock creates a strong user expectation of quick access. A dashboard that requires scrolling three times to find key information is hostile to a user working in a shared-device environment.

📥

Transcript download as a primary action, not a setting

In the FRD, transcript download was listed as a sub-item under "More." The design promotes it to the primary CTA on the Results screen. Transcripts are frequently needed for JAMB, WAEC, and university applications — moments of urgency where a student needs to act quickly. Burying the download in a settings menu creates friction precisely when the user has the least patience for it.

Results

What changes when school information is accessible

MSP shifts the default mode for school information from "drive to school and ask" to "open the app and check." This isn't a marginal improvement — it removes a structural friction point that affects every parent and student at every end-of-term across the country. When parents can see their outstanding fees without needing to call the bursar, payment rates improve. When students can see their CA scores during the term, they have something actionable to respond to.

The parent multi-ward view addresses a real complexity that most school apps ignore: Nigerian families frequently have children at different institutions, each with separate fee structures, term calendars, and result systems. Building this at the app layer — without requiring schools to integrate with each other — is a design decision that makes MSP deployable without institutional coordination.

What I'd do differently

Honest reflections

  • Attendance tracking was under-designedThe FRD lists attendance tracking as a core value proposition on the onboarding screen. The design delivers it as a data point on the dashboard but doesn't give it a dedicated screen with trend data, daily view, or absence alerts to parents. For a feature positioned as a key differentiator, this deserved more surface area.
  • The e-learning module was left as a placeholderE-learning appears in the bottom menu but was never scoped for the first phase. This is technically honest but creates a confusing expectation — a student who taps "E-learning" and hits a "coming soon" screen loses trust in the app's completeness. The entry point should not exist until the content does.
  • No notification designThe dashboard shows a bell icon. The FRD implies notifications exist. But the notification model — when they fire, what they contain, how they're prioritised — was never designed. A school app that sends a notification for every fee reminder, result release, and attendance event would quickly become noise. The filtering logic is a design problem, not just a backend one.
  • PTA levy payment flow was specified but not designedPay PTA levy appears as a menu item in both student and parent flows. This implies an in-app payment integration — a significant UX undertaking involving payment provider selection, receipt generation, and error handling. Specifying it without designing it creates an implementation gap that typically surfaces as a degraded experience at launch.
